places.name: Monaco

visits.text: Reach the Rock of Monaco-Ville via the historic lanes and the Saint-Martin gardens. This is where the Grimaldis took the fortress in 1297, disguised as Franciscan monks—an anecdote perhaps legendary, but which justifies the family motto: 'With God's Help.' The Notre-Dame-Immaculée Cathedral houses the tombs of the princes and Grace Kelly. The Palace esplanade offers a panorama over the principality and the harbor.

The Oceanographic Museum is one of the world's most important marine museums. Founded in 1889 by Prince Albert I—nicknamed the 'Prince of the Seas' for his 28 scientific expeditions—it was inaugurated in 1910. Its 85-meter-high building is constructed on the cliff face in neo-Baroque style. Jacques Cousteau was its director from 1957 to 1988. Its aquariums house more than 6,000 specimens of 350 species. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

Casino Square and the Monte-Carlo Casino: Belle Époque masterpiece designed by Charles Garnier (the architect of the Paris Opera) and inaugurated in 1863. The facades and vestibule can be visited freely. The grand gaming hall is accessible in the evening (ID required, smart dress code). Even if you don't enter, the square deserves a stop for its gardens and palaces.

activities.text: Stroll along Port Hercule: view the yachts (some exceed 100 meters) and spot the Formula 1 circuit that runs through the city streets every May. The track passes by the casino corner and the tunnel under the rock—one of the rare urban circuits where speed reaches 290 km/h.
Exotic Gardens and the Observatory Cave: the garden perched 100 meters above the city offers a 360° view over the principality. The adjacent prehistoric cave has yielded bones 200,000 years old.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland
